title: Tension Pneumothorax
objectives: Recognize clinical signs of tension pneumothorax
Understand the pathophysiology of hemodynamic collapse
Manage definitive treatment with tube thoracostomy

sections.title: Definition and Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**Tension Pneumothorax:**
Air enters the pleural space through a one-way valve mechanism and cannot escape, causing progressive accumulation.

**Mechanism of Deterioration:**
1. Air accumulates in pleural space
2. Ipsilateral lung collapses
3. Mediastinum shifts to contralateral side
4. Contralateral lung compressed
5. Great vessels kinked → ↓ venous return
6. Cardiac output falls → Obstructive shock
7. Death if untreated

**Key Concept:**
This is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Do NOT wait for imaging in an unstable patient.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Classic Triad:**
1. Hypotension
2. Distended neck veins (JVD)
3. Absent breath sounds on affected side

**Additional Signs:**
• Tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side (late sign)
• Hyperresonance to percussion
• Tachycardia, tachypnea
• Cyanosis
• Decreased oxygen saturation
• Agitation, altered mental status

**In Ventilated Patients:**
• Sudden increase in airway pressures
• Hypoxia, hypotension
• Difficulty bagging
• Cardiac arrest (PEA)

**Beware:**
• JVD may be absent in hypovolemic patients
• Tracheal deviation is often absent or late
• Breath sounds can be misleading in noisy environments

sections.title: Causes
sections.content:
**Traumatic:**
• Penetrating chest trauma
• Blunt chest trauma with rib fractures
• Iatrogenic (central line placement, thoracentesis, positive pressure ventilation)

**Spontaneous:**
• Rupture of bleb/bulla (often tall, thin young males)
• COPD, asthma exacerbation
• Cystic fibrosis
• Pneumonia with necrotizing infection

**Ventilator-Associated:**
• High PEEP
• High tidal volumes
• Barotrauma in ARDS

**Clinical Pearl:**
Any pneumothorax can become tension in a patient on positive pressure ventilation.

sections.title: Immediate Management: Needle Decompression
sections.content:
**Indication:**
Clinical diagnosis of tension pneumothorax with hemodynamic instability

**Equipment:**
• 14-16 gauge needle, at least 5 cm (2 inches) long
• In obese patients: 8 cm needle may be needed

**Traditional Site (2nd ICS):**
• 2nd intercostal space
• Midclavicular line
• Ipsilateral to absent breath sounds
• Insert above the 3rd rib (avoid neurovascular bundle)

**Alternative Site (4th-5th ICS):**
• 4th-5th intercostal space
• Anterior axillary line
• May have higher success rate in obese patients
• Less overlying tissue

**Technique:**
1. Prep skin if time allows
2. Insert needle perpendicular to chest wall
3. Advance until air rushes out (hiss)
4. Leave catheter in place, remove needle
5. Prepare for chest tube

sections.title: Definitive Treatment: Tube Thoracostomy
sections.content:
**Needle decompression is a bridge, not definitive treatment.**

**Chest Tube Insertion:**
• Location: 4th-5th ICS, anterior to mid-axillary line ("safe triangle")
• Size: 28-32 Fr for trauma, 24-28 Fr for spontaneous

**Steps:**
1. Position patient (arm above head)
2. Prep and drape
3. Local anesthesia
4. Incision at rib below insertion site
5. Blunt dissection over top of rib
6. Finger sweep to confirm pleural space
7. Insert tube, direct posteriorly and superiorly
8. Connect to underwater seal/suction
9. Secure and confirm placement

**Confirmation:**
• Fogging of tube with respiration
• Bubbling in chamber (air leak)
• Chest X-ray

sections.title: Special Scenarios
sections.content:
**Cardiac Arrest from Tension PTX:**
• Consider in any PEA arrest, especially if:
  - History of chest trauma
  - Central line placed
  - On mechanical ventilation
• Bilateral needle decompression may be performed

**Ventilated Patient:**
• High index of suspicion with sudden deterioration
• May need to disconnect ventilator briefly
• Chest tube definitive treatment

**Open Pneumothorax:**
• "Sucking chest wound"
• Cover with occlusive dressing taped on 3 sides
• Allows air out, prevents air in
• Chest tube on opposite side of wound

keyTakeaways: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis—do not delay for imaging
Classic triad: hypotension, JVD, absent breath sounds
Needle decompression is temporizing; chest tube is definitive
Consider in any PEA arrest with appropriate history
Any pneumothorax can become tension with positive pressure ventilation

references.citation: American College of Surgeons. Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) Student Course Manual, 10th Edition
Leigh-Smith S, Harris T. Tension Pneumothorax: Time for a Re-think? Emerg Med J. 2005;22:8-16
Laan DV, Ber Gerritsen M, Brouwer R, et al. Chest Wall Thickness at Potential Sites for Needle Decompression. Injury. 2016;47(4):797-800
references.keyContent: Evidence for lateral vs anterior approach

reviewQuestions.question: A trauma patient becomes hypotensive with distended neck veins and absent breath sounds on the right. What is the immediate next step?
reviewQuestions.explanation: This is classic tension pneumothorax presentation. Immediate needle decompression is required. Do not delay for imaging in an unstable patient.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the preferred site for needle decompression in an obese patient?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The 4th-5th ICS anterior axillary line has less overlying tissue and may have higher success rates in obese patients where standard needles may not reach the pleural space at the 2nd ICS.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected tension pneumothorax?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in tension pneumothorax. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of tension pneumothorax. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing tension pneumothorax, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
